lesson.title: Model Selection & Diagnostics
lesson.body: Information criteria balance model fit against complexity. AIC = −2logL + 2k, BIC = −2logL + k·ln(n). BIC penalizes complexity more, tends to select simpler models. Forecast accuracy metrics: MAE = mean|e_t|, RMSE = √(mean e²_t), MAPE = mean|e_t/x_t|·100%. Residual diagnostics check that residuals resemble white noise using the Ljung-Box test.

rooms.question: Between two models with the same log-likelihood, AIC selects:
answers: The one with fewer parameters (lower k)
rooms.explanation: AIC = −2logL + 2k. Same log-likelihood means lower AIC for smaller k. AIC penalizes extra parameters.

rooms.question: RMSE gives higher penalty to large errors than MAE because it:
rooms.explanation: RMSE = √(Σe²/n). Squaring amplifies large errors, making RMSE sensitive to outliers compared to MAE.

rooms.question: The Ljung-Box test statistic Q = n(n+2)Σ[ρ̂(k)²/(n-k)] tests:
answers: Whether first m autocorrelations of residuals are jointly zero
rooms.explanation: Ljung-Box tests H₀: ρ(1) = ... = ρ(m) = 0. Failure to reject suggests residuals are white noise — a sign of good model fit.

rooms.question: Order the residual diagnostic checks after fitting ARIMA:
items: Plot residuals over time (look for patterns/outliers)
Plot ACF of residuals (should be near zero for all lags)
Assess normality via QQ-plot or histogram
rooms.explanation: Time plot reveals heteroscedasticity or outliers; ACF checks autocorrelation; Ljung-Box formalizes it; QQ-plot checks distributional assumptions.

rooms.question: MAPE is inappropriate for forecasting when:
answers: The series contains zeros or values close to zero
rooms.explanation: MAPE = mean|e_t/x_t|·100% divides by actual values. If x_t = 0 or near zero, MAPE explodes or is undefined.

boss.name: The Information Criterion
boss.taunt: More parameters always improve fit. But I see through your overfitting.

questions.question: Why does BIC tend to select simpler models than AIC?
answers: BIC's penalty k·ln(n) > 2k for n > e² ≈ 7.4
AIC penalizes squared k, BIC penalizes k
questions.explanation: BIC penalty = k·ln(n) vs AIC penalty = 2k. For n ≥ 8, ln(n) > 2, so BIC penalizes complexity more, favoring parsimony.

questions.question: Walk-forward (rolling) validation in time series means:
answers: Training on past, testing on the next period, then extending the window
Randomly splitting the series into train/test
questions.explanation: Walk-forward validation respects the temporal ordering — no future data leaks into training. It gives realistic out-of-sample forecasting accuracy.

questions.question: Model A has AIC=250, BIC=270. Model B has AIC=255, BIC=260. Based on these:
answers: A is preferred by AIC; B is preferred by BIC — results conflict
questions.explanation: Lower is better for both. A wins on AIC (250 < 255); B wins on BIC (260 < 270). This conflict suggests model A has more parameters that AIC tolerates but BIC does not.
